notes.note: Heading: Cicero to Acilius the Proconsul, greetings. The recipient is M'. Acilius Glabrio, proconsul of Sicily 46--45 BC; not to be confused with the earlier M'. Glabrione who presided at the Verres trial (70 BC). Three successive letters (13.30--32) are addressed to him as Cicero commends different Sicilian connections.

notes.anchor: in company with the other Neapolitans he was made a Roman citizen, and is a decurion at Naples
notes.note: Lucius Manlius Sosis had been enrolled in the municipium of Neapolis before the lex Iulia of 90 BC extended citizenship to Italian allies; when citizenship came, he and other enrolees of municipal status were naturalised en bloc. Decurion (decurio) was the rank of a local council-member (ordo decurionum), the municipal equivalent of a Roman senator.

notes.anchor: before the citizenship was given to the Italian allies and the Latins
notes.note: The Social War (91--87 BC) ended with Rome extending full citizenship to allied Italian communities via the leges Iulia (90 BC) and Plautia Papiria (89 BC). Sosis's prior enrolment at Neapolis as a Latin-status decurion meant he was caught up in the mass enfranchisement of that municipium's registered inhabitants.

notes.anchor: business of long standing in his old province of Sicily
notes.note: The phrase sua Sicilia (his old Sicily) implies prior residence or economic interest in the island; men of Catina with Neapolitan municipal ties often had landed or commercial interests in Sicily, and such claims could require the proconsul's adjudication.
